mainEntity.name: How often should I have my dryer vent cleaned?

acceptedAnswer.text: The National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) recommends having your dryer vent professionally cleaned at least once per year. However, several factors can shorten that interval and call for more frequent cleanings: households that run 4 or more loads of laundry per week; long or complex vent runs (over 10 feet, or with multiple bends/elbows); homes with pets that shed heavily; dryers located far from an exterior wall (often basements or interior closets); and older dryers, which tend to produce more lint. During your service, we'll evaluate your vent configuration, lint accumulation rate, and usage patterns, then recommend a cleaning schedule tailored to your household — typically every 6, 12, or 18 months.

mainEntity.name: How long does dryer vent cleaning take?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most residential dryer vent cleanings are completed in 30 to 60 minutes, though the exact time depends on a few variables: the total length of the vent run (longer runs take more time to clear); the number of bends, elbows, or transitions in the ductwork; the severity of lint buildup (heavily clogged vents require more passes); the vent termination location (roof vs. wall vs. soffit); and the accessibility of the dryer and exterior vent cap. Our technicians arrive with all the necessary equipment, work efficiently, protect your floors and laundry area, and leave the space cleaner than we found it. In most cases, you'll be back to doing laundry the same day.

mainEntity.name: What if my dryer vent goes through the wall or roof?

acceptedAnswer.text: No problem at all — we routinely clean every type of vent configuration found in residential homes, including: short horizontal runs through an exterior wall; long horizontal runs through basements, crawl spaces, or interior walls; vertical runs that travel up through the attic; rooftop terminations (we have ladders and rooftop-rated equipment); multi-story homes with vents routed through several floors; and soffit and side-wall vent caps. We use rotary brush systems with flexible rods that navigate elbows and long runs, paired with high-powered vacuums that capture lint at the source. We clear the entire vent end-to-end and verify proper airflow before we leave.

mainEntity.name: Can I clean my dryer vent myself?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cleaning the lint trap after every load is something every homeowner should do — but the interior duct system is a different story. Here's why most homeowners are better off hiring a professional: consumer-grade brushes typically can't reach deep enough into long or angled vents; DIY cleaning often pushes lint further into the ductwork instead of removing it; disconnecting and reconnecting the vent improperly can create gas, fire, or moisture risks; roof and second-story vents require safe ladder access and the right equipment; and without a proper inspection, you won't catch crushed ducts, animal nests, or damaged vent caps. Our technicians bring commercial rotary equipment, HEPA-grade vacuums, and a trained eye for hazards — ensuring the vent is fully cleared and safely reconnected.

mainEntity.name: Can a clogged dryer vent really cause a house fire?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — and it's a serious, well-documented risk. According to FEMA and the National Fire Protection Association, U.S. fire departments respond to roughly 13,000–15,000 dryer-related fires every year, resulting in deaths, injuries, and more than $200 million in property damage annually. The single leading cause is failure to clean the dryer vent. Here's how it happens: lint is highly flammable and accumulates inside the vent over time; restricted airflow forces the dryer to run hotter and longer; excess heat builds up inside the drum and ductwork; a spark, an overheated heating element, or a hot lint particle can ignite the trapped lint; and once ignited, fire travels quickly through the duct and can spread into walls, attics, or the laundry room. Warning signs that your fire risk is elevated include: clothes taking much longer than usual to dry; the dryer feeling excessively hot to the touch; a burning smell during or after a cycle; visible lint around the exterior vent opening; and the dryer shutting off mid-cycle. The good news is that an annual professional cleaning is the single most effective way to eliminate this risk. Blackburns clears the full vent run from the dryer to the exterior termination, inspects the ductwork and vent cap for damage, and verifies proper airflow before we leave — so you can run your dryer with complete peace of mind.
